title: Distributed Quantum Circuit Optimisation: Evaluating Global and Local encodings
abstract: As distributed quantum architectures begin to emerge, understanding the interaction between quantum circuit optimisation and circuit partitioning becomes increasingly important. In this work, we study how circuit optimisation influences distributed quantum workloads under system-level trade-offs. We compare three compilation strategies (global optimisation, local optimisation, and a hybrid approach) across a large benchmark suite of quantum algorithms. Using telegate-based partitioning, we evaluate the resulting distributed circuits in terms of gate counts, circuit depth, the number of induced non-local gates, and compilation overhead, thereby approximating computational, communication, and classical preprocessing costs. Our results show that circuit optimisation does not uniformly benefit distributed execution. Global optimisation minimises computational resources and achieves the lowest compilation overhead. Local optimisation can reduce communication cost even though it is not explicitly communication-aware. The hybrid strategy can simultaneously reduce both computational and communication overhead, but at the expense of significantly increased compilation time.
summary: This paper evaluates how different ways of applying circuit optimization affect the performance of distributed quantum computers. It finds that the best strategy depends on whether the primary bottleneck is the number of gates, the amount of inter-QPU communication, or the time required to compile the circuit.
main_problem: Investigating how different quantum circuit optimization strategies (global, local, and hybrid) impact the trade-offs between computational costs and communication costs in distributed quantum computing architectures.
main_result: No single optimization strategy is universally superior; while global optimization minimizes computational resources and overhead, local optimization can reduce communication costs, and hybrid strategies can reduce both at the expense of significantly higher compilation time.
method: The authors compared three compilation strategies (global, local, and hybrid) using the MQT Bench suite of 1,943 circuits, employing KaHyPar for balanced hypergraph partitioning and Qiskit's transpiler for optimization.
model_or_system: A distributed quantum computing (DQC) architecture consisting of multiple interconnected Quantum Processing Units (QPUs) using a fully interconnected network model where cross-partition operations are implemented via teleportation-based telegates.
key_observables: Circuit depth, gate counts (1Q, 2Q, 3Q), number of induced non-local gates (communication cost), and classical compilation overhead/runtime.
important_parameters: Circuit size (ranging from 2 to 130 qubits), optimization levels, and the balance between computational, communication, and preprocessing costs.
main_assumptions: Assumes a fully interconnected network topology, ignores path length effects, assumes all non-local gates have equal cost, and assumes no additional qubit overhead for communication.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares computational resources (gate counts and depth) across strategies as circuit size increases; Figure 2 shows the advantage of global/hybrid encodings in reducing communication costs at larger scales; Table I provides compilation time statistics.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of distributed circuit optimization, describes the network and optimization models, details the benchmarking methodology, presents comparative results for computational and communication costs, and concludes with a discussion on the trade-offs between the studied strategies.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Computational resource comparison across optimi- sation encoding strategies after distribution. Global shown in yellow, Local in blue, Hybrid in pink and Non-optimised in green.
Figure 2: Mean inter-QPU communication cost under different optimisation encodings.
